content.problemStatement:
Time-series data is data recorded **at different points in time**, where the **sequence of values is meaningful**.


**Common Examples**
- Daily temperature readings
- Stock prices recorded every minute
- Monthly sales numbers
- Website visitors per hour

**Why Order Matters**
Consider the sequence:
[10, 12, 11, 14]
This sequence describes a pattern **only because** the values appear in this order.
If the same numbers are **rearranged**, the meaning **changes**. Time-series data is defined by this dependence on **order**.

---
At this point, you might be wondering how this is differ from **tabular data**.
Let’s see the difference.

**Time-Series vs. Tabular Data**
| Tabular Data | Time-Series Data |
|-------------|------------------|
| Row order is irrelevant | Order carries meaning |
| Each row is independent | Values depend on past values |
| No notion of time | Time is explicit or implicit |
| Example: exam scores | Example: daily temperature |


Next, we will see how an **MLP** interprets **time-series** data.
